text: Da Vinci single site cholecystectomy unedited console time. Many people believe that robotic surgery adds significant time to the otherwise efficient procedure of laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Here we'll show the unedited console time demonstrating that the time for surgical dissection of the gallbladder and its removal are similar to that for a standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon begins dissection of Calot's triangle using robotic instruments after placing the single site port and appropriate trocars.

The bedside surgical assistant uses a grasper to retract the fundus of the gallbladder and liver superiorly. On the right of the screen, a Maryland Grasper and Dissector in arm number two is actively dissecting tissue, while a Cobra Grasper in arm number one on the left is grasping the base of the gallbladder or Hartman's pouch.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate tissue layers around the gallbladder, using robotic instruments visible in the surgical field.
Notice that the picture produced by the laparoscopic camera is very stable in this case.

This is in sharp contrast to a SILS procedure where movement of the instruments produces significant movement in the vision or the field seen by the laparoscopic camera. In this case, the patient's anatomy is normal

The surgeon utilizes the flexible single site instruments, which can cross each other when necessary, to perform dissection maneuvers. Despite the complexity of these movements, the laparoscopic camera maintains a stable view of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissecting the cystic duct using primarily blunt dissection with a Maryland dissector, while the bedside assistant retracts the gallbladder fundus and liver superiorly.

The single-site instruments are visible, demonstrating mobility and proficiency in grasping and retracting tissues. Despite lacking articulation or wrist action, the instruments efficiently and safely create windows around anatomical structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection using single-site instruments, which are positioned similarly to those in a standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The graspers and dissectors manipulate the tissues effectively despite the lack of wrist articulation, providing a stable view akin to a standard laparoscopic approach.
The surgeon clips the fatty lymphatic tissues dissected from the anterior surface of the cystic artery using Hemlock clips to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon withdraws the visual field using the robotic surgical platform, providing a wider view of the operative area. The surgeon controls both the camera and the operative instruments, allowing for immediate adjustments to the visual field.

The surgeon inspects the positioning of the hemlock clips, ensuring they are fully locked. A cautery hook is then used to divide the tissues, achieving hemostasis without placing a clip on the gallbladder side.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery device to perform blunt dissection around the cystic duct lymph node, aiming to identify the cystic artery.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery device during the dissection to achieve hemostasis, effectively performing blunt dissection on the tissue.

Calot's triangle is clearly delineated, and windows have been created behind the cystic duct and artery. Hemlock clips are applied to control both the cystic duct and the cystic artery, ensuring hemostasis.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery using Hemlock clips, ensuring secure ligation. Two clips are placed on the cystic duct for internal retention, and one clip is used to ligate the base of the gallbladder, optimizing visualization of the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses scissors to divide the cystic duct, carefully maneuvering the blades between the closely placed Hemlock clips.
The surgeon places two Hemlock clips on the cystic artery and uses cautery to control back bleeding. The hook cautery device is then used to divide the cystic artery.
The surgeon uses the hook cautery device to continue dissecting and removing the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully separating it from the surrounding liver tissue.

The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and cautery dissection to clearly define the plane between the gallbladder and the liver bed, carefully avoiding penetration into either structure to prevent bile spillage and bleeding.

The semi-rigid single site robotic instruments are visible passing through the curving metal trochars into the abdomen. As the surgeon reaches the top of the gallbladder, more of the instrument is exposed, becoming less rigid and demonstrating instrument whip as tension is relieved upon complete tissue division.

The surgeon uses cautery near the liver, resulting in some past pointing onto the liver surface. The semi-rigid robotic instrument exhibits whip movement, requiring careful attention around delicate structures like the hepatic duct, common duct, and duodenum at the base of the gallbladder.

The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder using single-site robotic instruments to spread it out, providing adequate exposure of the posterior wall for dissection. The instruments' flexibility allows for effective maneuvering of the gallbladder from side to side.

The robotic camera provides high-definition visibility, aiding in the dissection by clearly delineating the plane between the liver and gallbladder. The gallbladder is nearly completely dissected from the liver, allowing inspection of the liver bed for hemostasis, with some areas cauterized. The clips are inspected and found secure, leading to the amputation of the gallbladder from the liver, completing the dissection.

The assistant uses a grasper to hold the base of the gallbladder. The gallbladder, along with the instruments, trocars, and single-site port, are removed through the umbilical incision, marking the end of the procedure.
